data.title: BlackBerry: The Company Has Bounced, Built Back Better
data.description:
Investment thesis

Once a formidable player in the multibillion-dollar mobile phone business, BlackBerry Limited (NYSE: NYSE:BB) saw its fortune and prospects take a significant hit. The struggles in taking on Samsung and Apple saw the company lose touch with consumers, even when producing some of the most sought-after and secure mobile phones.

After years of struggles and underperformance, BlackBerry has rediscovered itself. Its stock rallying by more than 100% last year and gaining 56% year to date is a testament to growing confidence in BlackBerry's long-term prospects and growth metrics.

BlackBerry's redesigned focus is the secret to its comeback. Having struggled to compete on the hardware side of the business, the company has revitalized its growth prospects with a focus on software. Consequently, it has become a major force in the Internet of Things ("IOT") and cybersecurity. With the industry prospect to be analyzed below, as well as the current under-valuation, I give the company a Buy rating.

Company background

Cybersecurity accounts for the biggest share of BlackBerry's core business with a $435 million annual run rate and a 55% gross margins.

Internet of Things, with a $245 million annual run rate, generates about 80% in gross margins, with IP licensing coming in third with a $20 million annual run rate and 60% gross margins.

Its cutting-edge driver-assistance platforms and infotainment systems are setting the standard for connected cars in the future. As autonomous driving technology advances, BlackBerry's software platform is becoming increasingly popular with automakers who want strong safety measures.

The company boasts a robust cybersecurity solution, embedded systems, and tracking technology portfolio. Under the leadership of John Chen, the Canadian tech giant is solely focused on reshaping the digital future through software solutions.

QNX Software Growth Prospects

QNX is one of the company's fastest-growing business segments, providing highly secure software for automobiles, medical devices, robotics, and industrial embedded systems. As of the fourth quarter of last year, QNX software was in over 255 million vehicles and more than 68% of electric cars. The fact that 24 of the top 25 electric vehicle manufacturers use the software affirms how well-positioned BlackBerry is for the next ten years as cloud computing becomes crucial for driving a software-defined future.

The Canadian company plans to offer a platform for automobiles that combines a large portion of the various computer hardware and software inside cars. John Wall, the COO of QNX, claims that this innovation will allow the company to increase its clients' average revenue significantly. BlackBerry might also consider charging subscription fees for services like security-related upgrades that can be added to car computer systems via over-the-air updates.

For the quarter that ended November, QNX unit's revenue was up 13% to $62 million compared to the previous quarter. EBITDA excluding some items also increased 38% to $18 million, affirming robust growth. BlackBerry plans to expand QNX's exposure to other sectors to generate more revenue beyond the automotive space.

"We see a lot of opportunity, especially with some of the bigger players in the industrial automation market. Robotics is an exciting market," QNX COO John Wall said.

Part of the QNX expansion plan will revolve around a strategic partnership with India's Pi Square Technologies. The two plan to provide a free, non-commercial version of its development platform to establish QNX as a standard in the automotive and IOT software sectors.

Secure Communications Lucrative Contracts

Secure Communications is BlackBerry's second business segment, which provides security technologies for protecting data and communications networks. It offers SecuSUITE, a cybersecurity solution that protects networks from various cybersecurity threats. Consequently, BlackBerry has secured deals with government agencies, strengthening its revenue base.

BlackBerry's Secure Communications portfolio includes the crucial events management platform AtHoc, the most popular product in the federal government and utilized by numerous federal agencies. It also provides SecuSmart, an encrypted voice solution that the US military, government agencies, and the Secret Service utilize. Lastly, the data sent to individual smartphones is managed by the company's Unified Endpoint Management ("UEM") solution.

Revenue in the Secure Communications segment has been growing steadily as BlackBerry reaps the rewards of its ongoing contract with financial services companies and government agencies.

BlackBerry Radar is one of BlackBerry's leading software solutions focused on providing advanced asset-tracking technology. It's also tailored to enhance efficiency across logistics and supply chain networks. The solution increasingly strengthens the Canadian company's growth prospects in the multibillion-dollar supply chain industry.

BlackBerry is also pursuing growth opportunities around quantum and blockchain technologies. It has already started integrating blockchain technology to enhance supply chain networks. It also leverages quantum computing to advance the encryption of its software solutions. Focusing on the two advanced technologies could be the catalyst for strengthening the company's growth metrics in the tech sector and strengthening its prospects in the market.

Improving Profit Margins

The sale of Cylance's endpoint security assets to Arctic Wolf for $160 million positions BlackBerry to focus on its core Secure Communication business. The fact that the segment consists of BlackBerry UEM, BlackBerry AtHoc, and BlackBerry SecuSUITE means the Canadian company will continue servicing its lucrative contracts with government agencies and financial services.

Similarly, the sale relieves BlackBerry of dealing with a unit that generated negative earnings of about $50 million annually. Given that the Secure Communication unit generated a positive EBITDA of $50 million, BB remains well positioned to strengthen its profit margins following the divestment of the sale of Cylance, and the additional money raised should enable the company to keep expanding its IoT operations and look for possible acquisitions that might improve its standing in the market.

BlackBerry is already considered a profitable company following the sale of Cylance. The company generated positive earnings from continuing operations of $12 million for the November quarter, expected to improve following the sale.

The QNX software business is also expected to impact profit margins positively. The unit's backlog has grown at a 33% compound annual growth rate. The company's Chief Financial Officer, Tim Foote, has already indicated that the significant backlog levels will bolster the company's profits in the next two to three years.

BlackBerry Revitalized Growth Prospects

BlackBerry's transition from a popular mobile phone company to a heavyweight in cybersecurity and automotive is gaining traction. The strategic shift places it at the forefront of emerging technologies, especially cybersecurity, automotive, and Internet of Things domains.

With a history of successful innovations, BlackBerry is leveraging its rich history to redefine its future on the software front. Ongoing developments and strategic partnerships highlight its potential to be a significant player in the future tech landscape. Likewise, the drive for safe, connected technologies worldwide underlines BB's stock as a viable option for long-term growth.

BlackBerry's market projections point to a bright future. Its AI-based cybersecurity solutions are becoming more popular due to increased demand for strong security measures and worldwide cybersecurity threats. BlackBerry's improving profit margins, robust growth outlook on the QNX unit, and the potential benefits of its Secure Communications business affirm long-term prospects.

Valuation

With the growth prospects in mind, we compare Blackberry with its peers in both the cybersecurity sector and the SaaS sector. In terms of multiples, Blackberry is still trading as an emerging, early software company. It's P/S ratio is significantly lower than the leaders in cybersecurity, despite having a growing positive cash flow and decent EBITDA.

With this in mind, the one-year target P/S ratio is at around 7x-10x, while the EV/EBITDA ratio is at around 25x-30x. As such, my target price for the company ranges from $5.9 - 6.7, which has an upside of more than 15%.

Investment risks

  • Technology and competition: In the cybersecurity field, competitions are fierce. Existing players such as PaloAlto Network and CloudFlare are established in the sector and competing with them requires time and resources, which may not yield effective results
  • Geopolitical tensions: BlackBerry is developing its business in China as discussed. With the new administration being unpredictable in foreign trade policy, and the retaliation from China, cross-border businesses like Blackberry may be impacted
  • Liquidity concerns: The company has a debt to cash ratio of lower than 1, signaling that it may face liquidity issues should the company needed more investment in its new strategy

data.title: Kratos Stock Is Flying Too High, Time To Sell Before The Bubble Bursts
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My investment thesis is very simple: Kratos (NASDAQ:KTOS) is both the most expensive company on the market and among its competitors in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA, and at the same time, it is one of the lowest–margin companies in the industry, with weak business growth rates, unstable financial situation, and value growth prospects - this simply cannot be.

  • The average P/E (TTM) among competitors is 58x, while Kratos' P/E (TTM) is as much as 272x;
  • The average EV/EBITDA (TTM) among competitors is 19x, while Kratos EV/EBITDA (TTM) is as much as 44x;

I believe that the company's shares need to be shorted, and their fair value is 34% lower than current values. I am assigning Kratos a "Sell" rating with a target price of $18 per share.

Introduction

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. is a large military technology company engaged in the development and manufacture of advanced systems and solutions for the U.S. Department of Defense and for the commercial sectors. The key activity of Kratos is the creation of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS), hypersonic and missile technologies, cybersecurity, satellite communications and advanced military solutions. Kratos specializes in providing innovative, but at the same time low-cost solutions, which makes it a niche company in the field of developing solutions for the American military-industrial complex. The company develops and implements new technologies for the U.S. Department of Defense, intelligence agencies, NASA, as well as major defense contractors and technology companies.

Kratos' business model is as follows: the company develops, manufactures and sells high-tech products and software for the needs of the defense and commercial sectors. The company's revenue is generated through government contracts, commercial orders, and long-term program agreements. The main sources of revenue include fixed production contracts (70% in 2023), actual cost and commission contracts (24%), and time and materials contracts (6%). 69% of the company's total revenue in 2023 was provided by orders from the US Department of Defense, NASA, the Missile Defense Agency, the Space Command and other government agencies, while the remaining 31% was provided by large private companies.

Kratos' development strategy is aimed at strengthening its position in key technology segments, expanding partnerships with major defense contractors, and increasing its share in fast-growing areas such as hypersonic technology, cybersecurity, satellite systems, and autonomous combat platforms. The company adheres to the "first to market" strategy, striving to be a leader in the development of advanced technologies and provide competitive advantages due to low-cost and fast development cycle. Kratos is also actively expanding its international presence: in 2025, it is planned to open new production facilities in Israel and India, which will strengthen its position in the global defense technology market.

Competitors

I believe that a comprehensive analysis of the company cannot be carried out without examining the industry itself in which Kratos operates. The company is considered to belong to the Aerospace and Defense industry. However, the industry itself is too vast and has a large number of segments within it. If we talk about the industry as a whole, the Aerospace and Defense Industry can be described as an oligopoly with elements of monopolistic competition in certain segments. It is important to understand that the main client of this industry is the government represented by the US Department of Defense, NASA, the Missile Defense Agency, the US Intelligence Agency and other major defense contractors. This industry has a limited number of large players, which is typical of an oligopoly, but it is divided into segments where smaller players operate. The market leaders are Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin, General Dynamics, Raytheon Technologies, BAE Systems, L3Harris, Boeing, General Atomics and others. These companies have significant financial, technological and production resources, which makes it extremely difficult for new players to enter the market. Kratos is not able to fully compete with these giants. Government orders are often awarded through tenders, but due to high requirements, contracts with long-term commitments, and the specifics of national security, the market remains highly concentrated in the hands of several companies.

At the same time, Kratos competes in certain segments such as hypersonic technologies, autonomous unmanned systems, satellite solutions, and electromagnetic weapons. Monopolistic competition operates in these segments. The main competitors here are Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, AeroVironment, Intuitive Machines, Hexcel Corporation, and in fact these are the key competitors of Kratos. If we summarize all these companies as one segment from the Aerospace and Defense industry, then this entire segment can be estimated at 10.47 billion dollars. Kratos' share in this segment is estimated at only 11%, while the largest player is Spirit AeroSystems with a 62% share.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in lower production costs and faster development time for new solutions. Kratos focuses on more affordable and mass-scale solutions. This gives it a niche competitive advantage, but competition remains fierce, as large defense contractors have the financial and technological resources to displace smaller players from certain niches in the long run. As a result, Kratos remains a weak player both in the overall industry and in its individual segments, lacking pricing power, and therefore Kratos is unable to increase its marginality and business growth rates at the expense of competitors.

Thus, the US defense industry is an oligopoly in which several large corporations control the main military programs and production of equipment. However, there is an element of monopolistic competition in innovative sectors such as unmanned systems, hypersonic technologies, and satellite communications, where Kratos offers more affordable and flexible solutions. This gives the company the opportunity to grow, but also exposes it to the risk of being ousted by more powerful players.

Financials

In the third quarter of 2024, the company's financial performance grew modestly compared to the third quarter of 2023. The company also revised its forecasts for key financial statements in a negative direction, especially in terms of operating profit. Management explains the deterioration in the forecast by a combination of several factors, including delays in deliveries, rising personnel costs and the impact of the Continuing Resolution Authority (CRA), which limits the possibility of obtaining new contracts and increasing financing for current ones.

According to the results of the third quarter, revenue increased by 0.5% compared to the same period in 2023 and amounted to $275.9 million. Revenue dynamics across the company's various business lines were mixed. The main contribution to revenue growth was made by the Unmanned Systems (KUS) division, which increased its revenues by 8.7% to $64.2 million in 2024. Other business areas of the company also showed positive dynamics, however, this growth was significantly offset by a drop in revenue in the space and satellite segment, where revenues decreased by $24.2 million. The reason for the drop in revenue is delays on the part of OEMs in the production and supply of software-defined satellites, which slowed down the receipt of new orders and the implementation of existing contracts.

The average annual growth rate of total revenue (CAGR) between 2018 and 2024 was about 10%. Management expects revenue growth of 10% in 2025, which is in line with my forecast. I expect revenue to grow by 10.91% to 1.275 billion rubles. The main contribution to growth is likely to come from large long-term contracts already concluded in 2024, which ensure a stable flow of orders. In particular, this is a 1.45 billion MACH-TB 2.0 contract.

The cost of sales includes the cost of production, SG&A, and 2-3% of all revenue the company directs to R&D. The cost increase in 2024 was mainly due to investments in technology development, staff growth, and high costs for equipment and customer support.

More and more of the company's costs are accounted for by R&D because the company continues to develop its product line and increase the staff of engineers. As a result, I expect the company to maintain its R&D spending level of about 3% of total revenue in 2025.

Kratos management expects EBITDA in the range of $102-107 million by the end of the year, which coincides with my forecast of $104 million in 2024. At the same time, I expect a reduction in EBITDA by the end of 2025 due to a reduction in EBITDA margin to 7.44%. My expectations are supported by the weak growth rate of the company's business, increased staff and R&D costs, as well as the company's position in the market, which does not allow it to keep margins at a stable level (as I said, the company's market share is small, there are many competitors, and they are strong, and Kratos does not have pricing power). The average annual EBITDA growth rate from 2018 to 2024 is 13.67%. According to my forecast, the company is likely to earn about $94.8 million in EBITDA in 2025.

Net profit for the year 2024 is likely to amount to about $15-16 million, potentially jumping by 553% year-on-year. In my opinion, the company would be able to increase its net profit margin to 1.3% from 0.23% in 2023 due to new contracts, particularly the MACH-TB 2.0 contract. And the return on equity (ROE) is about 1.34%. I expect net profit to grow to $21 million in 2025. Thus, we see that the company is running a very low-profit business, which may tell us either that management is not developing the business efficiently, or that the market is so competitive that Kratos has to sell its developments as cheaply as possible.

Reducing the debt burden remains the only positive development for the company. By the end of 2024, Debt to Equity decreased to 0.30x, indicating a decrease in dependence on borrowed funds and a strengthening of the capital structure. Net Debt/EBITDA, which peaked at 8.32x in 2022, decreased to negative values, which confirms a significant improvement in the financial situation. The Interest Coverage Ratio remains at a comfortable level of 1.51x, demonstrating sustainability in the payment of interest expenses. The growth of the Current Ratio to 3.22x highlights the increased liquidity and financial flexibility, which can allow the company to continue investment programs without risks to financial stability. The weighted average interest rate on the company's loans depends on the leverage level and amounts to SOFR + 1.25%-2.25%. To reduce interest rate risks in 2023, Kratos fixed the rate at 3.721% through a swap contract valid until May 2026.

Thus, Kratos' financial position is stable, but there are no great prospects for improvement. In fact, the company's business is low-margin and at the same time requires a constant increase in investments in R&D in order to remain competitive. Yes, indeed, the company has deleveraged over the past 3 years and significantly reduced its debt burden, but this business definitely does not look like P/E 272x from a financial point of view.

Valuation

Despite the fact that Kratos is shamelessly expensive by all multipliers, many analysts still recommend this promotion for purchase. It's a mystery to me how you can acquire a fairly mediocre business for 272 profits. And even despite the recent 20% stock price correction, Kratos remains the most expensive company in the industry and in its segment. Today, Kratos is trading at a 42% premium to its historical P/E averages and 7% on the EV/EBITDA multiplier. My thesis is that the company is inadequately expensive, no matter what you compare it to – peers or historical averages.

For comparison, I took Kratos' key competitors in the segment of developing cheap drones, or companies relatively similar to it from the Aerospace and Defense industry. In terms of P/E (TTM), Kratos is the most expensive among all, and in terms of EV/EBITDA, it is one of the most expensive. At the same time, it is one of the lowest-margin companies in the industry with the lowest growth rates.

The key advantage remains the absence of net debt, whereas some competitors have a significantly higher debt burden. At the same time, the company also shows one of the lowest ROE (1.34%), which indicates inefficient capital management. Collectively, Kratos remains an overvalued asset with low business growth rates, low margins, and controversial drivers for development.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 50%), target value of P/E and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 25%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 10.26%.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 58x, and EV/EBITDA is 19.5x (I took the average values for competitors). According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of Kratos shares on the horizon of the year is $18 per share, which is 34% lower than the current price. Thus, I consider the company's shares to be highly overvalued at the moment and I believe that it is possible to look closely at the purchase only at a price below $18 per share.

Risks

In order to make my thesis about the revaluation of this company more convincing, I will list the key risks that the company may face and which may negatively affect the business and the price of its shares, respectively.

A key risk for Kratos is dependence on contracts with the U.S. government, which provide most of the revenue. Any changes in budget policy, cuts in defense spending, or delays in allocating funding could negatively affect Kratos' business, reduce new orders, and lead to revenue losses. In addition, the continued use of temporary budget resolutions (CRAs), which prohibit the conclusion of new contracts and increased funding for existing programs, poses a serious threat to business stability. In addition, the impact of politics, for example, possible budget changes under the new US president, can significantly change the balance of power among companies in the industry. The geopolitical tension and the war in Ukraine favored industry companies to earn more money due to the growth of the defense budget, as well as through the popularization of drones. Against this background, Kratos shares increased significantly by almost 4 times, and revenue increased by 41% during this period. However, with the coming to power of Donald Trump, who intends to reduce a large number of programs, including those related to the military-industrial complex, the geopolitical situation may calm down, and the war in Ukraine might come to an end sooner than expected. We see all these steps after the start of direct negotiations between the United States and Russia to defuse bilateral relations and establish peace in Ukraine. Trump also stated that he would propose to Russia and China to halve defense spending. All this may mean a reduction in revenue for companies in the industry, including Kratos. It is this risk that is currently the key for the company, and the probability of realizing this risk is extremely high.

High competition in the defense industry also poses a significant challenge. Kratos competes with both large system integrators such as Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman and Raytheon Technologies, as well as smaller innovative companies that are actively implementing new technologies. High competition leads to lower product prices, lower margins and a loss of market share, which we actually see in the company's financial statements.

Thus, each of the risks I have listed can negatively affect the stock price. At the same time, these risks are already partially realized, as a result of which the market reacts with a correction in the share price of Kratos.

Drivers for stock price growth

Despite all the risks I have listed, Kratos still has strengths that the company can take advantage of and that can boost its stock price. The main factor is the growth of the defense budgets of the United States and its allies, which leads to an increase in demand for unmanned systems, satellite technologies and cyber defense. Despite Trump's peaceful rhetoric, negotiations may fail, and the war in Ukraine may continue, while increasing competition with China may force the US government to continue building up defense budgets.

Recent major military operations have shown that drones are a key tool for effective modern warfare. It is highly likely that the largest countries will continue to invest in and expand their unmanned programs. The company has already demonstrated success in developing next-generation drones such as the XQ-58A Valkyrie and Air Wolf, which can be used in combat operations without risking pilots. Kratos participates in the Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) program, where the US Air Force is considering the possibility of large-scale deployment of unmanned fighter jets. If the company receives large contracts under this program, its shares can grow significantly.

The financial stability of Kratos is also an important argument for the growth of its shares. The company demonstrates stable revenue growth, improves profitability and reduces its debt burden, which makes it attractive for investment. In addition, it is actively involved in the process of consolidating the defense industry: Kratos can either become a takeover target for a large defense corporation, or acquire promising companies on its own, which can increase its strategic importance.

Conclusion

Thus, I believe that the share price of Kratos is extremely overvalued, even after a 20% drop, and I disagree with most analysts about the serious growth potential of the company's business. I recommend selling the company's shares and considering its possible purchase only at a price level below $18. Kratos is currently the most overvalued company in the sector, and at the same time the company with the lowest profitability and business growth rates. The company has significant risks that can negatively affect the business and its final assessment, so keeping Kratos or even buying it at this price is a big risk.

data.title: Snowball Your Passive Income: 2 High-Yield Dividend Stocks With Significant Growth
data.description:
Investing in dividend growth stocks is a great way to not only compound your wealth and achieve attractive total returns over the long term, but perhaps even better, it is a great way to achieve financial independence and sustain that financial independence over the long term. This is because if you can live off of your dividend income, you reduce your sequence of returns risk since dividends are much more stable and dependable than stock market performance, which can be very volatile from day to day, month to month, and year to year.

One of the reasons why dividend growth stocks are such a great way to invest for total returns is because companies that pay out attractive dividends are typically very profitable and also need to have a stable business model to be able to support that dividend. On top of that, if they grow their dividends year after year, it means that management is very effective at allocating capital because it is able to support paying out increasing distributions to shareholders over time. It is fairly easy to paint a much better picture than what is actually happening at a company with all sorts of fancy non-GAAP metrics, but actually delivering increasing amounts of cold, hard cash to shareholders year after year is something that cannot be faked. Additionally, a rising dividend payout typically means that earnings per share are growing as well, and therefore, intrinsic value per share is increasing. This tends to drive the stock price higher over time, which, combined with the attractive dividend payout, delivers strong total returns.

On the financial freedom front, when you combine an attractive current payout that meets your current expenses with a high dividend growth rate, your purchasing power not only tends to keep up with inflation but actually increases relative to inflation over time. This means that your standard of living can actually improve during retirement if you follow this strategy.

While some might describe the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) as a simple, diversified path to achieving financial freedom through dividend growth investing, we think there is a better way to do it. For sure, SCHD is a fine approach for passive investors who do not enjoy the art of investing, but it is best suited for those who do not want to manage their own portfolios, study companies, or value stocks. However, for those who want to achieve better total returns and higher current income, we believe that either supplementing SCHD with some individual high-yield, high-growth stocks or even building an entire portfolio of individual stocks, as we do, is the best approach.

I wrote more about this in a recent article, so for more detail on that, you can refer to it and some other high-growth, high-yield stocks here. But in the meantime, here are two high-growth, big-dividend stocks that, I believe, are great vehicles for a portfolio that, with or without SCHD, can help you achieve your financial independence and total return goals over the long term.

High-Growth Big Dividend Stock #1

The first one I am going to talk about today is Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). BEP is a renewable power production and development company that is well-diversified across a broad range of technologies and geographies.

While many have put renewable power-and BEP by extension-in the penalty box right now due to President Trump's declared aversion to wind power, the reality is that wind only makes up about one-fifth of BEP's total portfolio, as well as its development pipeline. Additionally, only about half of its FFO comes from the United States. Therefore, a significant portion of its wind business is actually outside of the U.S., and nearly half of its cash flow comes from its crown jewel hydro portfolio.

As a result, BEP is much more than a wind power company. In fact, it is much more than even a wind and solar company, as combined they only make up 37% of its total FFO. Instead, it is a global, diversified energy giant that generates a large portion of its cash flow from real estate-like, permanent hydro assets that are currently benefiting from strong recontracting tailwinds. On top of that, it has a high-growth nuclear reactor business and is also poised to benefit significantly from the AI boom. It has a major power purchase agreement signed with Microsoft (MSFT) to power its AI ambitions, and is signaling that it expects to sign similar deals with other hyperscalers in the future. Management is also seeing further upside potential to its deal with MSFT.

Combining that with its BBB+ credit rating, its $4.3 billion in available liquidity, an 11-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, and only 9% of its debt having recourse to the corporation-with the rest being asset-level non-recourse debt-it is clear that BEP is in a strong financial position. Additionally, its lengthy contract profile along with the fact that the vast majority of its cash flow is indexed to inflation, makes it well-positioned to thrive in a stagflationary environment.

Perhaps most exciting of all is that management expects a 10%+ annualized FFO per unit CAGR for the foreseeable future, driven by a combination of its contractual rent escalators, contractual power purchase agreement escalators, operational improvement plans, extensive development pipeline, and capital recycling program. BEP has also proven to be opportunistic in repurchasing its own units and remains dedicated to growing its distribution year after year at a pace that easily exceeds inflation, which it will likely continue to do for the foreseeable future.

When you combine its double-digit FFO per unit CAGR outlook with its 6.4% distribution yield, BEP's total return and income potential become quite clear. Compared to other blue-chip real asset businesses such as Realty Income (O), the yield is somewhat comparable, as O's dividend yield is near 6%. However, BEP's growth rate is more than double that of O's, making it a much more dynamic investment. Meanwhile, its defensive positioning is nearly as strong, its credit rating remains solid, and it is also backed by one of the world's largest alternative asset managers, Brookfield (BN)(BAM).

On top of that, it offers investors flexibility through both its K-1 issuing security - BEP - as well as an economically equivalent option through Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) for those who wish to avoid the K-1.

High-Growth Big Dividend Stock #2

Another high-growth, big-dividend stock that I like a lot right now is Amcor (AMCR). It is a diversified packaging company that is preparing to merge with Berry Global (BERY), a move expected to enhance its capabilities, scale, and supply chain. Ultimately, this merger aims to deliver substantial synergies and accelerated growth through their highly complementary portfolios and innovation platforms. As a result, management expects 35% adjusted cash earnings per share accretion by year three, they anticipate $650 million in synergies, an additional $280 million of one-time cash benefits, and ultimately $3 billion of annual cash flow available to reinvest in the business, on top of the $180 million R&D spend.

Revenue growth is expected to accelerate by at least 1% from current levels, and total annualized return expectations are projected to increase by 300 basis points annually. As illustrated in management's capital allocation plan, they expect to distribute capital roughly equally across three different avenues:

  1. $1 billion toward capital expenditures for reinvestments into the existing business to drive strong organic growth.
  2. $1 billion into acquisitions and share repurchases, allocated to the most opportunistic places based on market conditions.
  3. A little over $1 billion in annual dividend payouts. This dividend will likely increase over time as the company raises it, although much of that increase will likely be offset by share repurchases. As a result, investors can combine the company's current 5% dividend yield with its expected 10% to 15% EPS CAGR to achieve 15% to 20% annualized total shareholder returns.

Management believes the stock will eventually reprice to a 3% to 4% dividend yield over time, providing further upside potential for investors. When you factor in its strong investment-grade balance sheet-with leverage expected to fall to three times or below by the end of fiscal 2025-significant liquidity, and strong free cash flow generation, AMCR is competitively positioned with a well-diversified business model and strong growth potential and looks like a very dynamic dividend growth investment.
